description:
1. With your legs out in front of you, place a roll just under your ankle so your heel can move freely. Keeping your leg straight, pull your toes up toward you.
  2. Then push your foot down like you are pushing on a pedal.
  3. Then keeping your leg in one spot and only moving at your ankle, turn your foot outward (eversion).
  4. Then bring it inward (inversion).

Reps: 10-15 | Sets: 2-3 | Which Side: Both | Hold Time: 1-2 seconds | How Often: 2-3 times per day.
tags: dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, inversion, eversion, foot, ankle sprain, tendonitis

description:
Eversion - 

  1. With your legs out in front of you, place a roll just under your ankle so your heel can move freely. Wrap a resistive band around the ball of your foot with the knot on the inside, and then anchor it around the other foot.
  2. Keeping the leg straight, and only moving at the ankle, pull outward with your foot, and control it back in.

Inversion - 

  1. With your legs out in front of you, cross the leg of the ankle to want to exercise over the other leg. Put the band around the ball of your foot with the knot on the outside, and anchor the other end around the other foot.
  2. Keeping the leg straight, and only moving at the ankle, pull outward away from the other foot, and control it back in.

Plantarflexion - 

  1. With your legs out in front of you, place a roll just under your ankle so your heel can move freely.
  2. Trying to keep your leg straight, push your foot down like you are pushing on a pedal, and then back up.

Dorsiflexion - 

  1. With your legs out in front of you, place a roll just under your ankle so your heel can move freely. Place a resistive band around the top part of your foot, and anchor the band around something in front of your foot.
  2. Keeping your leg straight, pull your toes up towards your head. Control the band back down.

Reps: 10-15 | Sets: 2-3 | Which Side: Both | Hold Time: 1-2 seconds | How Often: 2-3 times per day
tags: resistance, resistance band, eversion, inversion, dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, ROM, strengthening, foot, ankle sprain, ankle fracture

description:

Adjust the crutches so your elbows are slightly bent at about 20 degrees. There should be about a 2 inch space between your armpits and the crutch. The crutches should not touch your armpits.

  2. Step through with the good leg, and continue this movement.

1. Lie on your stomach. Bring your ankles towards your bottom, and reach behind you grabbing your ankles.
  2. Push your ankles upwards bringing your shoulders and hips off the floor or bed until you feel a stretch in your hips, and hold the stretch.

Reps: 2-3 | Sets: 1-2 | Hold Time: 30 seconds | How Often: 2-3 times per day
tags: back, core, ROM, hip flexor strain, herniated disc, HNP

description:
Lie on your stomach.

  1. Prone Is - Put your arms straight out in front of you with your thumbs up (like an I). Keeping your arms straight, raise your arms up off the floor or bed.  
  2. Prone Ts - Put your arms straight out to the side of you with your thumbs up (like a T). Keeping your arms straight, raise your arms up off the floor or bed. 
  3. Prone Ws - Put your arms out to the side with your elbows bent and palms down (like a W). Raise your arms up off the floor or bed squeezing your shoulder blades together.
  4. Prone Ys - With your thumbs up, put your arms straight out in front of you at an angle (like a Y). Keeping your arms straight, raise your arms up off the floor or bed. 

Reps: 10-15 | Sets: 2-3 | Which Side: Both | Hold Time: 1-2 seconds | How Often: 2-3 times per day
tags: shoulder, neck, back, strengthening, ROM, RTC, rotator cuff, shoulder impingement, shoulder bursitis, shoulder tendonitis, labral repair, winged scapula

description:
Up with the good, down with the bad. The crutches always stay with the bad leg. You will need to hop up and down the steps since you are non weight-bearing.

To go up: Hop up with your good (or strong) leg, leaving the crutches on the lower step. Then bring the bad leg and the crutches to that step.

To go down: Bend the good leg enough where you can place the crutches on the next step, then hop down to that step.

description:
Up with the good, down with the bad. The crutches always follow the bad leg. You can put your foot down if you are partial weight-bearing (about 50% of weight).

To go up: Step up with your good (or strong) leg, leaving the crutches with the bad leg. Then bring the other leg and the crutches to that step using a step to pattern.

To go down: Step down with the bad (or weak) leg, bringing the crutches with it. Then bring the other leg to that step using a step to pattern.

description:

To go down: Step down with the bad (or weak) leg, bringing the crutch with it. Then bring the other leg to that step using a step to pattern.

description:
Use a chair or something sturdy to hold onto for balance.

Hip Flexion with resistive band with a chair -

  1. With the band anchored behind you and the band wrapped around your ankle, keep your leg straight and your knee locked out.  
  2. Keeping your back straight and upright, slowly kick forward, and come back.

Hip Extension with resistive band with a chair -

  1. With the band anchored in front of where you are standing and the band wrapped around your ankle, keep your leg straight and your knee locked out.  
  2. Keeping your back straight and upright, slowly kick your leg behind you, and come back.

Hip Abduction with resistive band with a chair -

  1. With the band anchored on the opposite side you are standing and the band wrapped around your ankle, keep your leg straight and your knee locked out.  
  2. Keeping your back straight and upright, slowly kick your leg out to the side, and come back.

Hip Adduction with resistive band with a chair -

  1. With the band anchored beside the leg you are exercising and the band wrapped around your ankle, keep your leg straight and your knee locked out.  
  2. Keeping your back straight and upright, slowly kick your leg across your body, and come back.

Reps: 10-15 | Sets: 2-3 | Which Side: Both | Hold Time: 1-2 seconds | How Often: 2-3 times per day
tags: hip, knee, strengthening, ROM, balance, back, TKA, ACL repair, OA, TKR, HNP, low back pain, LBP

description:
Use a sturdy chair or counter top if you need help with balance.  

Standing Hip Flexion with a chair -

  1. Keep your upper body straight, and try not to swing your body.
  2. Keep your knee straight and pull your toes up towards you. Bring your leg forward and slowly come back down.

 Standing Hip Extension with a chair -

  1. Keep your upper body straight, and try not to swing your body.
  2. Keep your knee straight and pull your toes up towards you. Bring your leg backwards and slowly come back. Try not to arch your back.

Standing Hip Abduction with a chair - 

  1. Keep your upper body straight, and try not to swing your body.
  2. Keep your knee straight and pull your toes up towards you. Bring your leg out to the side leading with your heel, and slowly come back down.

Standing Hip Adduction with a chair - 

  1. Keep your upper body straight, and try not to swing your body.
  2. Keep your knee straight and pull your toes up towards you. Bring your leg across your body and slowly come back.

Reps: 10-15 | Sets: 2-3 | Which Side: Both | Hold Time: 1-2 seconds | How Often: 2-3 times per day
tags: knee, leg, balance, strengthening, TKA, ACL repair, back, OA, TKR, HNP, low back pain, LBP

nom: Wrist Ulnar Deviation with Resistive Band
description:
1. Hold a resistive band in your hands with your thumbs inwards facing each other, and put some tension on the band.
  2. Turn your wrist outward away from the other hand and slowly come back in.

Reps: 10-15 | Sets: 2-3 | Which Side: Both | Hold Time: 1-2 seconds | How Often: 2-3 times per day
tags: resistance, resistance band, wrist, arm, strengthening,  RTC, rotator cuff, shoulder impingement, shoulder bursitis, shoulder tendonitis, labral repair, tennis elbow, golfers elbow, TFCC, DeQuervains
